Authority and Coordination Dynamics.label: The Bottleneck Leader
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.description: Centralizing leadership to manage decision overload leads to dependency.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.problem: In rapidly evolving environments, decision-making can become overwhelming. Individuals feel lost without clear direction.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.solution: Centralize power in a single leader who “keeps things organized,” which, however, creates stagnation, dependency, and risks burnout or authoritarianism.

Authority and Coordination Dynamics.label: The Gatekeeper Effect
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.description: Filtering access in growing organizations concentrates control.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.problem: As organizations expand, filtering access to key resources becomes challenging, leading to a few individuals or groups becoming de facto gatekeepers.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.solution: The attempt to maintain quality ends up restricting participation and innovation by concentrating control.

Authority and Coordination Dynamics.label: The Cult of the Founder
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.description: Charismatic leadership creates dependency and stifles succession.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.problem: When groups form around a charismatic leader, power becomes overly concentrated, stifling governance evolution and suppressing succession.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.solution: Dependency on a single figure limits fresh ideas and undermines distributed leadership.

Authority and Coordination Dynamics.label: Shared Stewardship
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.description: Rotates leadership roles to distribute power and enhance inclusivity.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.problem: Centralized leadership leads to burnout and exclusion by confining decision-making to a small group.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.solution: Rotate leadership roles and share responsibility across the community to distribute power, enhance resilience, and encourage inclusivity.

Authority and Coordination Dynamics.label: Consent, Not Consensus
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.description: Uses consent-based decision-making to balance inclusion with action.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.problem: Striving for full consensus can slow decisions and marginalize minority viewpoints, leading to ineffective, watered-down outcomes.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.solution: Adopt a consent-based approach—“good enough for now, safe enough to try”—to balance inclusivity with timely action.

Authority and Coordination Dynamics.label: Power as Resource, Not Authority
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.description: Reframes power as enabling collective action rather than control.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.problem: Viewing power solely as control creates hierarchical bottlenecks and undermines collaborative efforts.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.solution: Reframe power as a resource that supports collective action, care, and innovation rather than serving as a tool for domination.

Authority and Coordination Dynamics.label: Right Decision, Right Level
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.description: Aligns decision-making authority with the group best suited to act.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.problem: Decision-making at inappropriate levels creates bottlenecks or concentrates power inefficiently.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.solution: Align decision-making authority with the group or domain most equipped to handle it, ensuring responsiveness and contextual insight.

Authority and Coordination Dynamics.label: Naming the Invisible
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.description: Makes implicit dynamics explicit to clarify roles and accountability.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.problem: Hidden assumptions and unspoken agreements lead to confusion and misaligned expectations.
Authority and Coordination Dynamics.solution: Use visual tools and open dialogue to reveal and clarify invisible structures, roles, and accountability.

Handling Complexity.label: The Illusion of Simplicity
Handling Complexity.description: Oversimplification masks the true complexity of challenges.
Handling Complexity.problem: In managing complexity, decision-makers oversimplify issues, masking the intricate nature of systemic challenges.
Handling Complexity.solution: Warns against simplistic narratives that lead to ill-prepared strategies, failing to address real-world intricacies.

Handling Complexity.label: The Predictive Fallacy
Handling Complexity.description: Rigid long-term plans assume a predictable future, reducing flexibility.
Handling Complexity.problem: Relying on fixed, long-term plans under the assumption of predictability can cause inflexibility when circumstances change.
Handling Complexity.solution: Highlights the dangers of over-planning and stresses the need for adaptable, responsive governance practices.

Handling Complexity.label: Over-Standardization Trap
Handling Complexity.description: Uniform policies stifle local adaptation and innovation.
Handling Complexity.problem: Imposing one-size-fits-all frameworks across diverse contexts can stifle local creativity and responsiveness.
Handling Complexity.solution: Recognize that uniform approaches often fail to account for local nuances, thereby curtailing innovation and responsiveness.

Handling Complexity.label: Sense-Making First
Handling Complexity.description: Prioritizes deep understanding of context before acting.
Handling Complexity.problem: In the rush to act, groups may bypass a deep understanding of the complex systems they operate within.
Handling Complexity.solution: Prioritize comprehensive sense-making—using systems mapping, storytelling, etc.—to ensure decisions are contextually informed.

Handling Complexity.label: Hold Multiple Truths
Handling Complexity.description: Encourages acknowledging diverse, even conflicting, perspectives.
Handling Complexity.problem: Binary, black-and-white thinking limits understanding and leads to polarized, narrow problem-solving.
Handling Complexity.solution: Encourage the simultaneous acknowledgment of multiple, even conflicting, perspectives to enrich decision-making.

Handling Complexity.label: Patterns Before Solutions
Handling Complexity.description: Focuses on identifying recurring issues before jumping to action.
Handling Complexity.problem: Jumping straight to solutions without understanding underlying recurring issues results in misdiagnosed problems and ineffective interventions.
Handling Complexity.solution: Analyze recurring patterns to uncover root causes before committing to specific actions.

Handling Complexity.label: Start Small, Learn Fast
Handling Complexity.description: Promotes small, safe-to-fail experiments for rapid, iterative learning.
Handling Complexity.problem: Overly ambitious planning in complex environments can cause paralysis and miss opportunities for incremental learning.
Handling Complexity.solution: Implement small, safe-to-fail experiments that allow for rapid learning and iterative improvement.

Handling Complexity.label: Stay in Inquiry
Handling Complexity.description: Fosters a culture of continuous questioning and exploration.
Handling Complexity.problem: A rush to judgment may lead to decisions that ignore evolving circumstances, resulting in ineffective outcomes.
Handling Complexity.solution: Encourage continuous questioning and exploration to keep decisions responsive to change.

Handling Complexity.label: Stop, Reflect, Realign
Handling Complexity.description: Builds in regular pauses for reflection and course correction.
Handling Complexity.problem: Continuous forward momentum without reflection can lead to missed learning opportunities and necessary course corrections.
Handling Complexity.solution: Build regular pauses into the governance process for reflection and realignment, ensuring ongoing responsiveness and effectiveness.

Handling Complexity.label: Slowing Down to Go Faster
Handling Complexity.description: Advocates deliberate slowing to enable thoughtful analysis and sustainable outcomes.
Handling Complexity.problem: The pressure for immediate results often forces rushed, shallow decision-making that overlooks deeper issues.
Handling Complexity.solution: Deliberately slow down processes to allow for thoughtful analysis and more sustainable, long-term progress.

Transparency and Participation.label: The Black Box Decision
Transparency and Participation.description: Opaque decision-making erodes trust and excludes affected stakeholders.
Transparency and Participation.problem: When decisions are made behind closed doors, trust is eroded and those affected feel excluded.
Transparency and Participation.solution: A drive for efficiency at the expense of openness undermines accountability and inclusiveness.

Transparency and Participation.label: Participation as Performance
Transparency and Participation.description: Superficial participation creates an illusion of inclusion.
Transparency and Participation.problem: Superficial participatory processes give an appearance of inclusion while key decisions remain in the hands of a few.
Transparency and Participation.solution: Exposes how performative participation leads to disengagement and cynicism when genuine influence is lacking.

Transparency and Participation.label: The Token Inclusion Trap
Transparency and Participation.description: Symbolic inclusion without real power-sharing undermines engagement.
Transparency and Participation.problem: Inviting stakeholders only symbolically—without transferring real decision-making authority—leads to frustration and undermines trust.
Transparency and Participation.solution: True inclusion requires transferring substantive decision-making authority rather than mere symbolism.

Transparency and Participation.label: Making Power Visible
Transparency and Participation.description: Reveals hidden power dynamics to build trust and clarity.
Transparency and Participation.problem: Hidden power dynamics can allow inequity to fester and erode trust across the organization.
Transparency and Participation.solution: Actively reveal and clarify power structures via transparent role definitions, open decision-making, and visible accountability measures.

Transparency and Participation.label: Participation with Purpose
Transparency and Participation.description: Structures engagement to align with individuals’ strengths and impact.
Transparency and Participation.problem: Unstructured or superficial participation fails to tap into the full potential of a community’s skills and insights.
Transparency and Participation.solution: Design engagement processes that align participation with personal strengths and interests, ensuring meaningful and impactful contributions.

Transparency and Participation.label: Entry by Relationship
Transparency and Participation.description: Uses relational entry to foster organic, trust-based inclusion.
Transparency and Participation.problem: Formal, impersonal vetting processes can feel exclusionary and prevent valuable new perspectives from being integrated.
Transparency and Participation.solution: Prioritize relational entry methods that build trust through personal connections, welcoming new voices organically.

Transparency and Participation.label: Learn in Public
Transparency and Participation.description: Encourages sharing successes and setbacks to foster collective learning.
Transparency and Participation.problem: When failures and challenges are hidden, organizations miss valuable opportunities for collective learning and growth.
Transparency and Participation.solution: Share both successes and setbacks openly to build a culture of continuous learning and improvement.

Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.label: The Fragile Hierarchy
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.description: Rigid hierarchies hinder innovation and adaptation.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.problem: Rigid hierarchies become brittle when leaders and established structures resist change and dissent.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.solution: Highlights the risk of centralized power that stifles innovation and impedes adaptive change.

Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.label: The Vanity Metric Trap
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.description: Overemphasis on superficial metrics obscures real impact.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.problem: Focusing on superficial, quantifiable metrics can obscure deeper systemic issues and impede meaningful progress.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.solution: Warns against mistaking vanity metrics for genuine impact and urges a focus on substantive outcomes.

Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.label: Suppressing Dissent
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.description: Silencing dissent prevents the healthy challenge necessary for adaptation.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.problem: Treating internal disagreement as disruptive silences critical voices and stifles innovation.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.solution: Marginalizing dissent prevents adaptive governance; open dialogue is needed to challenge and improve systems.

Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.label: Reflect, Adapt, Iterate
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.description: Establishes rhythms of reflection and iteration to adapt to challenges.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.problem: Systems that ignore feedback become stagnant and unable to cope with evolving challenges.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.solution: Establish regular reflection and iteration based on feedback, enabling continuous adaptation.

Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.label: Story as Feedback
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.description: Uses storytelling to capture qualitative insights alongside data.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.problem: Relying solely on quantitative data can miss the nuanced, qualitative dynamics of organizational life.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.solution: Incorporate storytelling to capture rich, qualitative feedback that enhances the decision-making process.

Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.label: Accountability as Care
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.description: Reframes accountability as a supportive, caring practice.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.problem: Traditional, punitive accountability models can damage relationships and inhibit collaboration.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.solution: Reframe accountability as an act of care—emphasizing shared values and mutual responsibility—to foster a supportive, growth-oriented environment.

Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.label: From Ownership to Stewardship
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.description: Shifts focus from individual ownership to collective stewardship.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.problem: When work is “owned” by individuals, it can lead to silos and burnout rather than collective progress.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.solution: Promote a stewardship model where responsibility is shared, encouraging collaboration and a sense of collective ownership over outcomes.

Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.label: Rest as Governance
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.description: Integrates rest into governance to sustain creativity and trust.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.problem: Continuous pressure and overwork erode creativity, trust, and the overall effectiveness of governance systems.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.solution: Integrate practices that prioritize rest and regeneration as essential components of sustainable governance.

Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.label: Replenishment is a Practice
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.description: Normalizes regular replenishment to prevent burnout and sustain engagement.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.problem: Emotional and relational fatigue, if unaddressed, can lead to burnout and reduce organizational effectiveness.
Adaptability and Fitness to Changing Con.solution: Normalize regular replenishment through retreats, rituals, or deliberate pauses to sustain energy and engagement.

Boundaries and Inclusion.label: Impenetrable Boundaries
Boundaries and Inclusion.description: Rigid boundaries inhibit new participation and foster insularity.
Boundaries and Inclusion.problem: Overly rigid boundaries can inhibit new participation and foster insularity, limiting growth and innovation.
Boundaries and Inclusion.solution: Overprotecting group identity at the cost of openness leads to stagnation and groupthink.

Boundaries and Inclusion.label: Entry by Favoritism
Boundaries and Inclusion.description: Favoritism in entry processes undermines transparency and diversity.
Boundaries and Inclusion.problem: Selecting new members based on personal relationships or favoritism undermines transparency and curtails diversity.
Boundaries and Inclusion.solution: Exposes the risks of informal, exclusionary practices and calls for merit- and relationship-based entry processes.

Boundaries and Inclusion.label: Closed Networks
Boundaries and Inclusion.description: Exclusionary networks risk echo chambers and limit innovation.
Boundaries and Inclusion.problem: Insular networks that exclude external voices risk becoming echo chambers, reinforcing biases and limiting innovation.
Boundaries and Inclusion.solution: Highlights the dangers of maintaining overly exclusive networks that fail to adapt to external insights.

Boundaries and Inclusion.label: Permeable Boundaries
Boundaries and Inclusion.description: Creates flexible boundaries that welcome new ideas while preserving core values.
Boundaries and Inclusion.problem: Overly rigid boundaries hinder growth by preventing fresh perspectives from entering the group.
Boundaries and Inclusion.solution: Establish flexible boundaries that allow new ideas to enter while preserving essential values and focus.

Boundaries and Inclusion.label: Invite, Don’t Oblige
Boundaries and Inclusion.description: Encourages voluntary, meaningful engagement instead of forced participation.
Boundaries and Inclusion.problem: Forcing participation can lead to disengagement and resentment, reducing the quality of involvement.
Boundaries and Inclusion.solution: Create engagement processes that invite voluntary, meaningful contributions rather than mandating participation.

Boundaries and Inclusion.label: Holding the Edge
Boundaries and Inclusion.description: Develops spaces for external voices to interact with core processes.
Boundaries and Inclusion.problem: Organizations often struggle to integrate external perspectives without compromising internal cohesion.
Boundaries and Inclusion.solution: Develop “edge spaces” where outside voices can interact with core processes, bringing fresh insights while maintaining group integrity.

Boundaries and Inclusion.label: Trust is the Work
Boundaries and Inclusion.description: Emphasizes ongoing relational work as the foundation for effective governance.
Boundaries and Inclusion.problem: Focusing solely on measurable outputs neglects the relational work that builds trust within an organization.
Boundaries and Inclusion.solution: Make trust-building an explicit, ongoing practice by dedicating time and resources to relationship cultivation and transparent communication.
